html:
YEV v YEW

YEV v YEW
[2026] SGFC 83

Case Number:Divorce No 2690 of 2024 (Ancillary Matters)
Decision Date:25 June 2026
Tribunal/Court:Family Court
Coram: Kow Keng Siong
Counsel Name(s): Margaret Yeow Tin Tin, Louise Lim Yi Hui (M/s Hoh Law Corporation) for the Wife; Mohamed Fazal Muhammad Aadil bin Dafir (M/s I.R.B. Law LLP) for the Husband.
Parties: YEV — YEW

Family Law – Custody – Access – Child aged 14 – Whether access should be made subject to child's wishes

Family Law – Custody – Access – Drafting of access terms – Parents acrimonious and holding personal protection orders against each other – Whether access terms should be clear and structured rather than open-ended

Family Law – Maintenance – Wife – Factors relevant to fixing duration

Family Law – Maintenance – Wife – Whether maintenance to be paid as a lump sum or by periodic payments – Relevant considerations

Family Law – Maintenance – Backdating – Whether maintenance should be backdated – Relevant considerations

Family Law – Matrimonial assets – Whether asset a matrimonial asset – Husband's pre-marital CPF Ordinary Account moneys applied towards purchase of matrimonial home – Whether such moneys co-mingled and rendered matrimonial – Whether pre-marital Medisave and Special Account moneys remaining identifiable and excluded – Effect of closure of Special Account on member attaining 55 and transfer of moneys to Retirement Account – Section 112(10) Women's Charter 1961

Family Law – Matrimonial assets – Dissipation – Whether past dispositions to be added back to pool – Categories comprising ordinary spending, substantial unilateral spending against the backdrop of divorce, and deliberate diversion or concealment

Family Law – Matrimonial assets – Division – Single-income marriage – Broad-brush approach – Marriage of about 14½ years with wife as homemaker save for four years – Whether homemaker wife entitled to 30% or 35%

Family Law – Matrimonial assets – Division – Matrimonial home purchased substantially with husband's CPF moneys – CPF refund secured by charge under s 21B Central Provident Fund Act 1953 – Required CPF refund exceeding husband's share of home – Transfer of excess from husband's CPF account to wife's CPF account to give effect to the division – Sections 21B and 27B Central Provident Fund Act 1953

25 June 2026

District Judge Kow Keng Siong:

Introduction

1       The Plaintiff ("Wife") and the Defendant ("Husband") were married in March 2010. The Wife began divorce proceedings on 6 June 2024. The marriage ended on 22 October 2024, when the court granted an interim judgment for divorce ("interim judgment").

2       This judgment deals with the ancillary matters that remain to be decided following the divorce.

Arrangements concerning the son

3       I begin with the arrangements for the parties' son, who was born in June 2012.

Custody, care and control

4       The parties ask for (a) joint custody of the son and (b) care and control to the Wife.[note: 1] I approve these arrangements: see Annex 1A.

Access

Parties’ positions

5       The parties’ position on access changed over the course of the hearing.

(a)     In April 2026, the Wife proposed a detailed set of access terms ("Wife's original proposal"). These are set out in Annex 1B. One of them required the Husband's sister to stay away during access.[note: 2] The Husband accepted all the terms except this one, which he considered "unwarranted and excessive".[note: 3]

(b)     In May 2026, the Husband proposed dinner access on alternate weeks as an interim arrangement. He explained that he was living in his sibling's three-room HDB flat, and that he would seek overnight access once he was able to buy a flat of his own.[note: 4]

(c)     In June 2026, the Wife said that she was willing for the Husband to have "reasonable access to the child, provided that such access is subject to the child's wishes and availability" ("Wife's subsequent proposal"). She added that the Husband should arrange access with the son directly.[note: 5]

Applicable principles

6       Every decision the court makes about a child must begin and end with one question: what is in the child's best interests. That is the paramount consideration, and every other factor matters only so far as it bears on it. For a child entering the teenage years, the two things that matter most are stability in his or her daily life and a healthy relationship with both parents. An access order should be shaped to protect both: see e.g. WKM v WKN [2024] 1 SLR 158 ("WKM") at [94]; CX v CY (minor: custody and access) [2005] 3 SLR(R) 690 at [26]; VJM v VJL [2021] 5 SLR 1233 at [5].

7       In ensuring that the child's welfare is safeguarded in a workable access order, several factors are relevant, including the following.

(a)      The child's age and circumstances. These shape what kind and frequency of access is realistic and meaningful. A teenager's schooling and social life, for instance, call for different arrangements from those suited to a young child.

(b)      The relationship between the child and the access-parent. This indicates how much contact, and in what form, will be comfortable and beneficial for the child.

(c)      The relationship between the two parents. This is important because the quality of access in practice often turns on how well, or how poorly, the parents can cooperate in carrying it out.

8       When drafting the actual terms of an access order, two objectives are important.

(a)      Clarity. The terms must be precise enough – on matters such as timings and handover arrangements – to leave little room for dispute. Vague terms are an invitation to argument between the parents, and that conflict frequently spills over onto the child.

(b)      Flexibility. The terms must also be able to accommodate the changing needs and routine of a growing child. An order that is too rigid will soon become unworkable.

These two objectives frequently pull in different directions. The court's task is to strike a sensible balance between certainty and adaptability. Where that balance lies depends on the particular family, and in particular, on how cooperative the parents are likely to be towards each other.

My decision

A.    The relevant considerations

9       Three features of this case shape the access order that I make.

10      First, the son's age and stage of life. He is 14 and in Secondary 2. Two things follow from this.

(a)     He is old enough to have a developing sense of self, a need for privacy, and a legitimate claim to be taken seriously as an individual. An arrangement that disregards them risks not being truly child-centred.

(b)     His school life will be demanding. Homework, co-curricular activities ("CCA") and examinations are central to a Secondary 2 student's life. The order must work around these commitments.

11      Second, there had been issues between the son and the Husband.

(a)     The Wife had earlier applied, on the son's behalf, for a personal protection order ("PPO") against the Husband. I heard that application. I found that the Husband had (i) shouted at the son over a bowling ball that the son wanted to buy, (ii) accused him of lying about a matter that was in truth a dispute between the parents, and (iii) stopped him from closing his bedroom door while the Husband looked for documents in the room. I dismissed the application because the incidents were minor, infrequent, and did not indicate a risk of future family violence.

(b)     That dismissal does not make those incidents irrelevant here. They matter because they may colour how the son views his father. The access order should help to rebuild that relationship, not strain it.

12      Third, the circumstances of, and between, the parents.

(a)     The Husband currently lives with his sibling in a three-room HDB flat. That accommodation is not suited to overnight access, and the Husband himself intends to seek overnight access only once he has a flat of his own. I therefore defer overnight access, with liberty to the Husband to apply once his housing situation changes.

(b)     The Wife is a former cancer patient who has been out of the workforce for over a decade. She must now find employment. (For the reasons, see discussion on “spousal maintenance” below.) Raising a teenager alone in these circumstances will be demanding. The Husband's active involvement in the son's life can ease some of that burden – which is a reason to make his access regular and dependable.

(c)     Unfortunately, the parties' relationship is acrimonious; they hold PPOs against each other. Their need to communicate in order to arrange access is therefore a likely flashpoint. The order must reduce the occasions for friction between them.

B.    Assessment of the Wife’s subsequent proposal

13     The Wife's proposal – "reasonable access" that is "subject to the child's wishes and availability" – appears to be motivated by the son's welfare: to protect his studies and to be sensitive to his readiness to spend time with a father who has been harsh with him. These concerns are legitimate.

14     I am nonetheless unable to adopt the proposal for three reasons.

(a)     To make the Husband's access "subject to the son’s wishes" would be to make the child decide whether he will see his father. A 14-year-old should not have to carry the responsibility of making such a decision week after week – and to bear the possible guilt that the choice can bring: WKM at [43], [44] and [78].

(b)     Further, making access "subject to the son’s wishes" will in effect be giving the child a right of veto over access. This is not advisable as a 14-year-old may not yet have sufficient maturity to make rational decisions about whether he should, or should not, see his father. In this case, there is also the risk that such decisions may be shaped, consciously or otherwise, by the hostility between the Wife (the care-parent) and the Husband.

(c)     "Subject to availability" is, in any event, too vague to be workable here.

(i)       Between cooperative parents, such open-ended language is unobjectionable and may even be desirable. In TUV v TUW [2016] SGHCF 15 at [4] and [5], the High Court ordered "reasonable liberal access" to be "mutually arranged" by the parents. This was because the parents were willing to cooperate in enabling their children to spend time with both of them.

(ii)       The Husband and Wife are, however, not in that position. Each holds a PPO against the other. Where parents are likely to be hostile, the access terms should be clear and predictable rather than open-ended, because vagueness in such a case is an invitation to conflict: XZG v XZH [2026] SGFC 25 at [33]. An open-ended order is a convenience between cooperative parents and a battleground between hostile ones.

C.    Orders made

15     For these reasons, I find the Wife's subsequent proposal – "reasonable access" that is "subject to the child's wishes and availability" – to be inappropriate on the facts of this case.

16     A clearer and more structured set of terms is needed – one that provides the son regular and meaningful contact with the Husband, built around his studies and other genuine commitments, with enough precision to prevent disputes and enough flexibility to meet real needs as they arise.

17     With the above considerations in mind, and taking into account the Husband's indications (see [5(b)] above), I amend the terms of the Wife's original proposal and impose the access order, as amended, set out in Annex 1B.

Child maintenance

18     Next, I turn to the maintenance of the son.

Parties’ position

19     The parties' estimates of the son's monthly expenses are far apart. A detailed breakdown of these expenses is at Annex 2, columns (B) and (C).

Wife’s estimations

Husband’s estimation

$3,654/month[note: 6] (estimate in 2026)

$2,500/month[note: 7] (estimate in 2025)

$1,200/month[note: 8]



Interim maintenance order

20     The Husband supports his lower figure by pointing to a January 2025 interim maintenance order which the parties had entered into by consent. He submits that the order "provides a practical and realistic indication of [the son's] financial needs and the parties' present financial circumstances".[note: 9] He adds that the Wife should also work and meet part of the child’s expenses.[note: 10]

21     Under the interim maintenance order, the Husband had agreed to the following.

(a)      First, to pay $1,700/month to the Wife as interim maintenance for the son and herself. (Note. The order did not state how the amount of $1,700 is to be divided between the two of them.)

(b)      Second, and in addition, to pay directly –

(i)       the son's clothing, medical, school (fees and related items), allowance, insurance, transport, tuition and enrichment, spectacles and SIM-card expenses;

(ii)       $58/month towards the Wife’s handphone bill; and

(iii)       the outgoings on the matrimonial home – namely, Town Council service and conservancy charges, property tax, utilities, repairs and maintenance, and TV and internet charges. (Note. The Husband estimates these at about $361/month in total.[note: 11])

My decision

22     I am unable to accept either party's estimate of the son's expenses. The Wife's estimate is inflated. It includes items that are discretionary and others that are insufficiently explained. The Husband's estimate understates, and in some aspects omits altogether, several ordinary expenses that the son clearly requires.

23     The court's task is to fix a reasonable budget for the son – one that reflects his actual needs, the parties' respective means, and the practical realities of parenting after divorce. Having weighed these matters, I order that the parties maintain the son on terms as set out at Annex 1A. In summary –

(a)     The Husband shall pay the Wife $800/month as general maintenance for the son. (Note. For how this figure is computed, see Annex 2, column (D).)

(b)     In addition, the Husband shall bear directly the son's expenses set out in Annex 2, column (D). On the Husband's own estimates, these come to about $650/month:[note: 12] see the items in the grey cells in Annex 2, column (C).

(c)     The Wife shall bear the discretionary items identified in Annex 2, column (D). On the Wife's own estimates, these come to between $550 and $850/month: see Annex 2, column (B).

24     In making the above allocations, I am guided by the following three principles.

(a)     Both parents owe the son a duty to maintain him. What differs between them is the manner of maintenance, which should take into account each parent's means and capacity: WBU v WBT [2023] SGHCF 3 ("WBU") at [35] and [36]; YAM v YAN [2026] SGHCF 14 at [170].

(b)     The duty is to meet the son's reasonable needs. It does not require the Husband to fund every expenditure that the Wife considers desirable. In this regard, the fact that an expense was incurred during the marriage does not make it a reasonable maintenance expense after it.

(c)     If the Wife wishes to give the son more than is reasonably necessary, she remains free to do so: WBU at [9] and [18].

25     In structuring the order, I have combined the budget approach with direct payment approach. My reasons follow.

(a)      The budget. A fixed monthly sum sets a baseline for the son's reasonable expenses without the court having to dictate every item: WBU at [11]. This is appropriate because a child's day-to-day needs shift from month to month. It is neither realistic nor desirable for the parties to return to court each time the cost of food, transport or some incidental item moves modestly. A monthly budget spares them that.

(b)      The figure. I find that $800/month is a reasonable budget for the son's ordinary recurring expenses – food, dining out, clothing, haircuts, allowance and transport. I have rounded the computed figure modestly upward (Annex 2, column (D)). This is because maintenance should be practical and workable, not artificially precise. Its purpose is to give the Wife, as the care-parent, a stable and predictable sum to manage.

(c)      Direct payment of core expenses. I order the Husband to bear the son's educational, medical, insurance and other core expenses (Annex 2, column (D)) separately and directly, for two reasons. First, these expenses go to the son's development rather than his day-to-day upkeep. Second, the Husband already pays them directly under the interim maintenance order.

(d)      Discretionary items. I do not treat everything that the Wife claims as maintenance to be something that the Husband must fund. Payments for vitamin supplements, bowling enrichment, computer games, books, a new handphone and holidays are discretionary expenses. If the Wife wishes to provide them, she may do so from her own resources. Likewise, the Husband remains free to provide them during access or by agreement with the Wife. The order fixes a floor – not a ceiling – on what each parent must provide for the son.

(e)      Dental expenses. Necessary dental treatment is health-related and so is essential. However, on the Husband's evidence, he has already paid the son's dental expenses, and the parties agreed that he would not be liable for further such expenses. I therefore make no prospective order for ordinary dental expenses. This is without prejudice to both parents' obligation to act reasonably should the son require medically necessary dental treatment in future.

(f)      Household expenses. I disallow the Wife's claim for "future household expenses" of $370/month. This appears to refer to the household expenses that the Husband pays under the interim maintenance order: see [21(b)(iii)] above. These expenses come to about $361/month. Child maintenance is for the son. It is not a general subsidy of the care-parent's household, save to the extent an expense is shown to be properly and solely attributable to the son. These expenses are not, however, ignored. I have provided for them under spousal maintenance, so that they are met once, but not twice: see Annex 3, s/n 12.

26     Before moving to the next issue, I make three observations.

(a)     The order leaves the Husband bearing the larger and more essential part of the son's maintenance. In my view, this is fair. The Husband is in employment and earns a net income of $7,206/month[note: 13] (excluding bonuses). The Wife, on the other hand, is presently unemployed, though she has an earning capacity: see [103] below. Where one parent has income and the other, for now, has none, it is appropriate for the parent with means to carry the greater share, while the discretionary items are left to the Wife's choice.

(b)     The order is a framework to be applied sensibly. The parties remain free to agree on the provision of additional contributions for the son’s expenses.

(c)     The order may be varied if the circumstances change materially. If there is such a change, for instance in the son's needs or in either parent's means, either party may apply to vary the order. In the absence of such a change, the parties should not return to court over every fluctuation in the son's spending.

Division of matrimonial assets

27     I turn next to the division of the parties' matrimonial assets. This requires me to (a) identify the assets in existence at the operative date; (b) determine which of them are matrimonial assets; (c) value those assets; and (d) divide them justly and equitably. I take each step in turn.

Applicable principles

Step 1 – Identifying the assets

28     The first step is to identify the assets in existence at the date of interim judgment.

(a)     That date is the operative one because it marks the legal and practical end of the marriage. After it, the parties no longer build up matrimonial assets together as they did during the marriage: ARY v ARX [2016] 2 SLR 686 ("ARY") at [28] and [31] to [36]; BPC v BPB [2019] 1 SLR 608 ("BPC") at [26].

(b)     The court may depart from that date where the justice of the case requires – for example (i) where an earlier or later date better reflects when the marital partnership, in substance, ended; (ii) where one party has unfairly depleted the assets (for instance, through gambling or other dissipation); or (iii) where moving the date is needed to give effect to an adverse inference: ARY at [36]; BPC at [37] to [40].

Step 2 – Deciding which assets are matrimonial

29     The second step is to decide whether an identified asset is a matrimonial asset. Where there is a dispute, the following considerations are relevant.

(a)      The statutory definition. Section 112(10) of the Women's Charter 1961 ("Charter") defines a "matrimonial asset" in two limbs.

(i)       Assets acquired before the marriage by one or both parties if they were ordinarily used or enjoyed by the parties or their children while the parties were residing together for shelter, transportation, household, education, recreational, social or aesthetic purposes; or if they were substantially improved during the marriage by the other party or by both parties.

(ii)       Any other asset of any nature acquired during the marriage by one or both parties.

The provision excludes gifts and inheritances unless that asset is the matrimonial home, or unless it has been substantially improved during the marriage by the other party or by both parties.

(b)      Burden of proof. It is important to know who must prove what.

(i)       As a starting point, once a marriage is dissolved, the parties' assets are presumed matrimonial. A party who claims an asset is not matrimonial bears the burden of proving that, on the balance of probabilities: USB v USA [2020] 2 SLR 588 ("USB") at [31].

(ii)       That position is reversed where an asset is prima facie not matrimonial – for example, a clear gift or inheritance. Should this arise, the party asserting that the asset is matrimonial bears the burden of proving the facts that bring it within s 112(10): USB at [32].

30     The third step is to value the identified matrimonial assets.

(a)     The general rule is that assets are valued as at the date of the ancillary matters hearing, or as near to it as the evidence allows: BPC at [43]. The reason is that, once an asset is in the pool, both parties share in the fluctuations in its value when it is eventually divided at the ancillary matters hearing. This is so regardless of whether the asset is jointly or separately owned: BPC at [49] to [52].

(b)      The exception is for bank and CPF accounts, whose balances are generally taken as at the date of interim judgment. This is because the matrimonial asset is the money in the account, not the account itself. Money paid in after interim judgment is not a matrimonial asset: BUX v BUY [2019] SGHCF 4 at [4]; CVC v CVB [2023] SGHC(A) 28 at [55].

31     The final step is to divide the assets justly and equitably: s 112(1) and (2) of the Charter.

(a)     Here, it is common ground that this was a largely single-income marriage, with the Husband being the sole breadwinner.[note: 14] The Wife was a homemaker save for 2011 to 2014, when she earned about $1,700/month plus commission of about $600.[note: 15]

(b)     In single-income marriages, the court divides the assets using a broad-brush approach: TNL v TNK [2017] 1 SLR 609 ("TNL") at [43] to [46]. This approach involves three steps.

(i)        Start with the duration of the marriage. The longer the marriage, the greater the weight given to the parties' indirect contributions: ANJ v ANK [2015] 4 SLR 1043 ("ANJ") at [27].

(ii)        Calibrate against the precedents. The court then looks to the division ratios applied in comparable marriages. The following trends have been identified in TNL at [48] and BOR v BOS [2018] SGCA 78 ("BOR") at [111] and [113]:

Duration of marriage

% of matrimonial assets awarded to homemaker spouse

10 to 15 years

25% to 35%

15 to 18 years

35% to 40%

26 to 30 years or more

50%



It bears noting that the above ratios are simply guides. The court does not divide assets with mathematical precision. Its task is to ultimately arrive at a division that is just and equitable: NK v NL [2007] SGCA 35 at [28]; WBR v WBS [2024] SLR (FC) 128 at [90].

(iii)        Adjust for the parties' actual contributions. The court then adjusts the indicative ratio, where appropriate, to reflect what each party had in fact contributed. Relevant factors include the following.

(1)       Whether there were children and, if so, their number and ages. These factors indicate the extent and intensity of homemaking and caregiving.

(2)       Whether the homemaker had a high earning capacity that was given up for the marriage: WDO v WDP [2022] SGHCF 11 at [16]. This is important because such a spouse may have lost income that would otherwise have counted as a direct contribution under the structured approach (ANJ at [22]), and so be disadvantaged on division.

(3)       Whether the homemaker made meaningful direct financial contributions.

(4)       Whether the working spouse also contributed substantially to childcare and household work: DBA v DBB [2024] 1 SLR 459 at [19] to [22].

(5)       Whether the asset pool is exceptionally large or unusual: ANJ at [27(b)]; TNL at [52]; CLT v CLS [2021] SGHCF 29 at [79].

Disputed assets and dispositions

41     Next, I turn to a group of assets and dispositions that are hotly contested. Each party alleges that the other has "dissipated" matrimonial assets, has failed to account for the proceeds of various dispositions, and should have adverse inferences drawn against him or her.

Approach taken

42     In each case, the underlying question is the same: should a past disposition of matrimonial assets be added back to the pool for division? The answer depends on which of the following three categories the disposition falls into.

(a)      Category 1 – Ordinary spending (not added back). The first category is the use of matrimonial assets in the ordinary course of married life. Spouses in a functioning marriage spend to run the household, raise the children, invest, travel and maintain a lifestyle. Some of the spending may be unwise. Some investments may fail. This fact alone does not prove that there had been dissipation. In the absence of clear evidence, a court will not speculate that spending made in the distant past is improper and add that it back to the matrimonial pool. Parties are entitled to use their resources in the ordinary ways that functioning families do: UZN v UZM [2021] 1 SLR 426 ("UZN") at [66] to [70]; WOS v WOT [2024] 1 SLR 437 at [52]; WVS v WVT [2024] SGHC(A) 35 at [14] to [18]; WRX v WRY [2024] 1 SLR 851 ("WRX") at [25].

(b)      Category 2 – Substantial, unilateral spending against the backdrop of divorce (added back). The second category is the depletion of a matrimonial asset when divorce was imminent, or after interim judgment but before the ancillary matters were concluded. The amount is added back to the pool where (i) the spending was not for daily, run-of-the-mill expenses, (ii) the sum was substantial, (iii) the other spouse had a putative interest in the asset, and (iv) that spouse did not agree to the spending, before or after. This is so even if the spending was honest and well-intentioned. This is because once there is a prospect of divorce, the asset should have been preserved for division, and one spouse should not have spent it substantially and unilaterally without the other's consent: TNL at [24]; UZN at [62] to [65]; TOF v TOE [2021] 2 SLR 976 ("TOF") at [68] and [69]. Where the funds have already been spent, they are added back notionally – that is, the spending spouse is treated as having already received his or her share of the asset: TNL at [37].

(c)      Category 3 – Deliberate diversion or concealment (added back). The third category is the deliberate diversion or concealment of matrimonial assets to put them beyond the other spouse's reach: UZN at [66] to [68]. Indicators that a disposition is of such a nature include the following: (1) the asset is of significant value; (2) the disposition was close in time to the separation, divorce or ancillary hearing; (3) the counterparty is connected to the spending spouse; (4) there are no contemporaneous supporting documents; (5) the explanation for the disposition is inconsistent or absent; (6) the disposition makes no commercial sense; (7) the disposition departs from past practice; and (8) where a loan is alleged, repayment was never pursued. If diversion or concealment is proved and the value of the asset is known, that value is added back. If the value is unknown, the court may instead estimate it and add it back, or award the innocent spouse a larger share of the known assets: UZN at [28] to [35]; TOF at [66] and [67]; WRX at [38] to [41]; WTL v WTM [2024] SGHCF 40 ("WTL") at [59] to [62]; WZF v WZG [2025] 3 SLR 1219 at [58] to [61]; XIT v XIS [2026] SGHC(A) 2 at [45] to [49], [62] and [63].

43     Throughout, the legal burden of proving dissipation or concealment stays on the spouse who alleges it: ss 103, 105 and 108 of the Evidence Act 1893. That said, the following bears highlighting.

(a)      The evidential burden can shift. Once the accusing spouse makes out a prima facie case of a suspicious disposition involving a matrimonial asset, an evidential burden falls on the other spouse to explain the disposition. A spouse who should know where the asset went cannot simply respond to the allegation with silence, vagueness or bare assertion: UZN at [26] and [55]; TOF at [89] to [93]; WTL at [64] to [68].

(b)      Inadequate disclosure can lead to adverse inferences. The court may also draw an adverse inference of dissipation or concealment where a spouse has been selective or piecemeal in providing disclosure – or has refused to provide disclosure – of relevant records (such as bank statements) within his or her control: UZN at [18]; TOF at [64] to [67]; WRX at [38].

44     I now apply this approach to the disputed assets and dispositions in this case.

Joint POSB bank account (XXX262)

45      The asset. The parties hold a POSB account in their joint names ("joint account"). Its balances at the relevant dates were as follows.

 

Date

(Last transaction before the interim judgment)

$9.12



46      The Husband's submission. The Husband submits that the Wife had dissipated funds from the joint account. According to him, the Wife kept the passbook and ATM card throughout. From 2014, he paid his monthly salary and bonuses into the account to meet the Wife's and the household's expenses. Within two days of each deposit, she would withdraw almost the entire sum. There was, he submits, "no plausible reason" for a family of three to spend $4,500/month on groceries, the son's tuition and other daily expenses. This is especially so because he had separately paid the recurring household bills himself such as utilities, Town Council service and conservancy charges, phone and home internet, insurance premiums, property tax, HDB season parking, road tax, petrol and cashcard top-ups. When he asked the Wife to account for the withdrawals, her answers were unsatisfactory. She said, in broad terms, that the money went on general living, medical treatment, legal fees and family expenses, but gave no particulars and no "coherent tracing or documentary [support]" to show how the withdrawals were in fact spent.[note: 24]

47      The Wife’s explanation. The Wife explains that she had withdrawn the funds to meet the maintenance needs of the son and herself. The need to do so grew from mid-2024, when the Husband cut his deposits into the account from $4,500/month to between $1,500/month and $1,700/month.[note: 25]

48      My decision. The Husband's case, in essence, is that the Wife had spent the household money that he gave her. This is not dissipation. It is the use of matrimonial assets in the ordinary course of married life: see [42(a)] above. The legal burden of proving dissipation lay on the Husband: see [43] above. In my view, he has failed to discharge this burden. I accept the Wife's explanation that the withdrawn funds were predominantly used to meet the maintenance needs of the son and herself. These are my reasons.

(a)      The Husband has paid $4,500/month as household expenses for years. On his own evidence, the Husband had deposited about $4,500/month into the joint account for the Wife's and the household's expenses – and had done so for years before divorce was ever in contemplation. A husband cannot fix the household budget himself, pay it month after month for the better part of a decade, and then, once the marriage ends, claim that very spending as being implausible and demand that his wife produce receipts to justify it. What the Husband now calls dissipation was, on his own account, simply the arrangement by which this household had long been run.

(b)      The figure is reasonable. Spending about $4,500/month on the Wife, the son and the household is unremarkable. Setting aside the discretionary items for the son (such as health supplements, bowling classes, outings and holidays), the cost of maintaining the Wife and the son alone comes to about $3,250/month:

 

Maintenance for –

Amount

(1)

Wife

$1,800[note: 26]

(2)

Son

$1,450[note: 27]

(3)

Total

$3,250



If one were to add the Husband's share of the household expenses (e.g., food and groceries)[note: 28] and the son's discretionary expenses, the total would come to about the $4,500 that the Husband had been depositing each month. The arithmetic corroborates the Wife's account.[note: 29]

(c)      The reduction in the Husband's payments made the withdrawals necessary. It is not disputed that from June 2024, the Husband had cut his maintenance payments. A wife left to make up that shortfall is entitled to draw on the matrimonial funds available to her: see [126] below. In this case, the Wife’s use of funds in the joint account was a response to his reduction, not a diversion of the asset.

49     For these reasons, the Husband has not made out a prima facie case that the Wife dissipated funds from the joint account. The evidential burden did not shift to her, and the absence of itemised "tracing" for ordinary household spending is irrelevant. The account now holds a negligible value ($9.12). I exclude it from the matrimonial pool.

Wife’s “undisclosed assets” and joint account with the son (XXX001)

50      The Husband's case. The Husband submits that in her first affidavit of assets and means, the Wife had failed to disclose or account for matrimonial assets totalling $117,045.80 ("undisclosed assets").[note: 30] These consist of –

(a)     SGX bonds acquired in 2022, worth $25,000 (principal) plus $9,045.80 (proceeds);

(b)     Proceeds of an NTUC Income policy, surrendered in May 2025, which had been bought with a single premium of $60,000 in October 2020; and

(c)     Rental income of $23,000 from letting out a room in the matrimonial home between November 2022 and September 2024.[note: 31]

51      The Wife's explanation. The Wife's account of what became of each of these assets is as follows.[note: 32]

(a)      SGX bonds. The bond was redeemed in 2022. Of the $25,000 proceeds, she used $15,954.42 for her cancer-related TCM treatment and hospital check-ups. The remaining $9,045.58 was used to buy a second bond. That second bond was later redeemed, and the money paid into her OCBC joint account with the son (XXX001).[note: 33]

(b)      NTUC Income policy. The policy was surrendered in or around April 2025. It yielded about $58,827.94. The Wife applied the proceeds as follows.[note: 34]

(i)       $15,000 went to her TCM rehabilitation, family expenses and legal fees. (She did not say how much were the legal fees.)

(ii)       A further $35,000 had been set aside to repay a $35,000 loan from her brother. However, she later used it instead for "imminent family expenses and legal proceedings". (Again, she did not say how much were for legal fees.)

(iii)       The balance of $8,041.91 was paid into her joint account with the son. She agrees for this sum to be returned to the matrimonial pool.

(c)      Rental income. This too was paid into her joint account with the son (XXX001). Of it, $21,000 went on family expenses (including the son's enrichment classes and daily necessities). The remaining $2,000 was spent on transport relating to the divorce.[note: 35]

52      The Husband's response. The Husband asks the court to reject the Wife’s explanations as being unsupported by documents. He also points to the following as internal inconsistencies in her case.[note: 36]

(a)     In a letter dated 3 November 2025, the Wife's lawyers stated that the NTUC proceeds had been used to repay her brother's loan. However, in a later letter dated 25 November 2025, they said that her brother had allowed her to keep the money. Neither letter suggested that the proceeds had been used for maintenance.

(b)     The Wife's claim that she had to use her own funds to cover the maintenance shortfall is inconsistent with her case that (i) she was a housewife with no income and (ii) she had undisclosed assets of substantial value (i.e., the SGX bonds, the NTUC proceeds and the rental income).

53     The Husband adds that the Wife had planned the divorce well in advance. She had engaged divorce lawyers by the end of 2023 and served the papers on 6 June 2024 – one week before he turned 55 and became entitled to withdraw part of his CPF savings.

54      My decision. I find that the proceeds of the SGX bonds, the NTUC Income policy and the rental income are all matrimonial assets. Each was acquired during the marriage, long after the Wife became a full-time homemaker. Assets acquired during the marriage are presumed matrimonial, and the burden lay on the Wife to show otherwise: see [29(b)] above. In this regard, she led no evidence that any of them was acquired with her own, non-matrimonial, funds. Take the NTUC Income policy as an example. She did not explain how, as a homemaker, she had $60,000 to buy the policy. Her agreement to return the balance of the surrender proceeds ($8,041.91) to the pool only confirms that the policy was bought with matrimonial money.

55     The next question is how much of these matrimonial assets ($117,045.80 in total) must be returned to the pool. To address this question, I start with the position that since the dispositions were substantial and were made during marriage without the Husband's knowledge or consent, they should be added back to the matrimonial pool. I then proceed to deduct those dispositions which the Wife has shown should be excluded from the pool because they do not fall within [42(b)] and [42(c)]. In this regard, I find three dispositions to fall within this category.

(a)      Cancer treatment in 2022 ($15,954.42). Of the $25,000 from the SGX bonds, the Wife had used $15,954.42 for her cancer-related TCM treatment and hospital check-ups. That was in 2022, when divorce was not imminent. I find the use of the monies to be reasonable. There is no evidence that she was putting the money beyond the Husband's reach.

(b)      Coverage of the maintenance shortfall ($16,750). It is not disputed that, from mid-2024, the Husband had reduced his maintenance payments and so created a shortfall. I accept – and the bank records bear this out[note: 37] – that the funds in the Wife's joint account with the son (XXX001) came largely from the rental income and were spent largely on the son's enrichment classes and daily necessities. The Wife claims that she had used about $21,000 of those funds to make up the shortfall. On my own calculation, the shortfall should be lower – about $16,750: see [121] and [122] below. It is this lower sum that I deduct.

(c)      Live-cell therapy in December 2024 ($3,988). The Wife had also used $3,988 to pay for live-cell therapy.[note: 38] The evidence shows that at the material time, she was consulting doctors for her cancer.[note: 39] The Husband did not seriously dispute this, and I do not regard the payment as an improper disposition: see [42(b)] and [42(c)] above.

(d)      The result. Deducting these three sums from the $117,045.80 leaves $80,353.38 to be restored to the matrimonial pool:

56     One further item should be addressed, lest it be thought overlooked. About $4,074 of withdrawals from the Wife’s OCBC joint account with the son was paid to law firms. These payments appear to be unrelated to maintenance.[note: 40]

(a)     They were made between May 2023 (about a year before the divorce was commenced) and July 2024 (about a month after). There is no evidence that the Husband had consented to them, or that they were for family expenses.

(b)     Matrimonial assets should not be used to meet a party's personal legal costs, including the costs of the divorce: UZN at [45]; WRX at [25]. In principle, therefore, the sum of $4,074 ought to be returned to the pool.

(c)     The return has already been carried out. It is contained within the $80,353.38 to be restored to the pool. To add it back a second time would be double counting. I therefore make no separate addition for it.

A.    Do the balances contain pre-marital assets?

58      The Husband's submission. The Husband submits that $282,634.65 of these balances should be left out of the division. That sum was already in his CPF accounts on 19 March 2010 – the day before the marriage. Having been acquired before the marriage, he submits, it is not a matrimonial asset under s 112(10) of the Charter.[note: 42]

59      The Wife's submission. The Wife disagrees. During the marriage, the Husband had used his CPF savings towards the downpayment and the monthly instalments on the matrimonial home. This amounts to $279,539.72, according to the CPF letter dated 30 March 2025. She therefore submits that whatever pre-marital CPF monies that the Husband once had were, by now, used up, depleted. They had turned into matrimonial value (the home) or mixed with his post-marital CPF contributions – and thus can no longer be carved out.[note: 43]

60      My decision. The parties’ submissions raise a key issue: have the Husband's pre-marital CPF savings been absorbed into the matrimonial home and thus became matrimonial assets? My analysis is as follows.

(a)      The test and burden of proof. For each account, the question is whether the portion that the Husband seeks to exclude remains identifiable as a pre-marital asset – and was not used, depleted, transformed or co-mingled during the marriage. The burden of proving that lies on the Husband, as the party asserting that part of an asset should be left out of the pool: USB at [31].

(b)      Ordinary Account – Included. Ordinary Account savings may be applied to the purchase of an HDB flat: Central Provident Fund (Approved Housing Schemes) Regulations 1986.[note: 44] The Husband did precisely that. On the CPF records, he had applied $279,539.72 of his CPF monies to the purchase of the matrimonial home – a sum that exceeds the $191,234.03 of pre-marital Ordinary Account savings that he now seeks to exclude. He has produced no records showing that the home was paid for wholly out of post-marital CPF contributions, or that his pre-marital Ordinary Account balance was left untouched and separately identifiable. A reasonable inference is that the pre-marital Ordinary Account monies went into the home, and so were co-mingled and rendered matrimonial: WTS v WTR [2024] SGHCF 33 at [7]. The pre-marital funds in his Ordinary Account are thus not excluded.

(c)      Medisave Account – Excluded. Medisave savings cannot be used to buy an HDB flat. Accordingly, the Wife's co-mingling argument would not apply to them. Further, the Husband's pre-marital Medisave balance (19 March 2010) was $37,000, while his current Medisave balance is $75,471.21. There is no evidence that the Medisave account was ever drawn below $37,000. This is another reason why the $37,000 remains identifiable as a pre-marital asset and is thus excluded.

(d)      Special Account (now within the Retirement Account) – Excluded. Special Account savings, like Medisave savings, cannot be used to buy an HDB flat. The commingling argument thus has no application to them either. The one complication is that the Husband no longer has a Special Account. It was closed for members aged 55 and above in January 2025, and its savings were transferred into his Retirement Account. That closure does not change the position for three reasons.

(i)       The Husband's pre-marital Special Account balance (19 March 2010) was $54,400.62. The savings in this account are not freely withdrawable, and there is no evidence that this balance was withdrawn, invested and lost, or otherwise depleted during the marriage. If anything, his retirement savings have grown.

(ii)       The transfer of those savings into the Retirement Account, on the closure of the Special Account, was an administrative reallocation of one retirement account into another. It applied the savings to no matrimonial purpose, and changed nothing about their character. That the pre-marital Special Account savings now sit in the Retirement Account beside savings transferred from the Ordinary Account does not make the pre-marital portion unidentifiable: the pre-marital figure is fixed by the CPF records, and the current Retirement Account balance ($316,602.17) exceeds it. Pre-marital savings do not become matrimonial merely because a change in the CPF rules later moved them into a different account.

(iii)       To be clear, I exclude only the pre-marital Special Account portion of $54,400.62. Everything else in the Retirement Account – including savings derived from the Ordinary Account and from post-marital contributions – stays in the matrimonial pool.

I add, for completeness, that the position would be different had the evidence shown that the pre-marital Special Account savings had been invested under the CPF Investment Scheme, withdrawn, or otherwise dealt with during the marriage so that they could no longer be identified. There is no such evidence here.

61     In summary, under the CPF framework, only Ordinary Account savings may be used to buy an HDB flat. Medisave and Special Account savings cannot. What cannot be spent on the home cannot have been co-mingled in it. Accordingly, the Husband's CPF savings liable for division come to $473,821.69 minus ($54,400.62 + $37,000) = $382,421.07. This is made up of the whole of the Ordinary Account ($81,748.31), the Medisave Account less the pre-marital $37,000 (that is, $38,471.21), and the Retirement Account less the pre-marital Special Account portion of $54,400.62 (that is, $262,201.55).

B.    Should $25,000 be returned to the pool?

62      The Husband's position. In August 2024, the Husband transferred $25,000 from his CPF accounts into his POSB account (XXX838).[note: 45] He claims that the withdrawal was for family-related purposes.[note: 46]

63      The Wife's position. The Wife disputes this. She submits that there is no evidence to support the Husband's explanation, and that the $25,000 should be added back to the matrimonial pool.[note: 47]

64      My decision. I am unable to accept the Husband’s claim that the $25,000 was transferred for family-related purposes. These are my reasons.

(a)      The Husband did not need the money. When he transferred the $25,000 into his POSB account (XXX838) on 28 August 2024, that account already held $97,521.00.[note: 48] He was clearly not short of funds for the family. If anything, it was the reverse: he had extra funds because he had been reducing his maintenance payments to the Wife since June 2024: see [121(b)] below.

(b)      He never said what the $25,000 was for. The Husband has not identified any family expense, or even any category of expense, that called for a payment of $25,000.

(c)      The account records contradict him. Between 28 August 2024 (when the $25,000 was deposited into his POSB account) and 18 October 2024 (when a further $50,000 was deposited), the only substantial withdrawals from the account were three payments to "IRB" (totalling $5,831.50) and two maintenance payments (totalling $3,000). The rest involved much smaller amounts.[note: 49] Nothing in the records resembles $25,000 of family spending.

(d)      The transfer must be accounted for. $25,000 is a substantial sum. The Husband transferred it into his own personal account, after divorce had commenced, without telling the Wife. A disposition of this nature falls squarely within [42(b)] above.

65     Ordinarily, the consequence would be to add the $25,000 back to the Husband's CPF accounts for division. Here, this is unnecessary. Moving money from one of one's own accounts to another does not take it out of the matrimonial pool. It merely changes the pocket in which it sits. The $25,000 has not gone anywhere – it went into the POSB account, and it forms part of the $128,332.65 standing in that account at the date of interim judgment (22 October 2024), which I bring into the matrimonial pool below: see [66] to [68] below. To add the $25,000 back to the CPF accounts as well would be to count the same money twice. I therefore make no separate addition for it.

Husband’s POSB current account (XXX905)

69      The asset. The Husband held a POSB current account which he closed in November 2024.

70      The Husband’s explanation. He explains that the account was primarily used to issue cheques and not as a savings account.[note: 52]

71      The Wife’s claim. The Wife points out that the Husband had closed the account after the divorce had begun, and that he had produced no bank statement for it. She submits that he had diverted funds from the account, and that those funds should be returned to the matrimonial pool.[note: 53]

72      My decision. The burden of showing that the Husband had diverted matrimonial assets from this account lay on the Wife: see [43] above. I find that she has not discharged this burden. The Husband's explanation – that this was a current account used for issuing cheques and not maintained as a savings account – is plausible and unrebutted.

Husband’s joint POSB account with the son (XXX074)

74      The Wife's claim. The Wife submits that the account was set up to hold the son's red-packet money each year, saved for his future educational and personal expenses. She points out that the Husband's withdrawal of $5,400 emptied the account, and that this happened exactly a week before he filed his Counterclaim (Amendment No. 1). This, she claims, shows that he had deliberately put the $5,400 beyond her reach.[note: 55]

75      The Husband's explanation. The Husband submits that the account had held about $12,000 consisting of (a) $3,000 of the son's red packet money and (b) $9,000 from his own income and contributions. He claims that the Wife had withdrawn about $7,000 without consulting him. He subsequently withdrew about $5,000 to prevent further unilateral withdrawals. He is willing for $9,000 to be returned to the matrimonial pool.[note: 56]

76      My decision. I make no order in respect of this account. Both parties have already drawn broadly similar sums from it. Ordering them to return the money now – only to divide it again – would change nothing. Let me explain.

(a)     I accept as plausible the Husband's evidence that the Wife had withdrawn about $7,000 from the account. She complained about his withdrawal of $5,400 – but said nothing about an earlier withdrawal of $5,000 in July 2024. Had that $5,000 also been taken by the Husband, she would surely have asked for it back as well, just as she did for the $5,400. A reasonable explanation for her silence regarding the $5,000 withdrawal is that she had carried out the transaction.

(b)     Each party had withdrawn a broadly similar sum – the Wife about $7,000 and the Husband about $5,000. Between them, they had emptied an account of which only some $9,000 was matrimonial asset (the remaining $3,000 being the son's red packet money, which is not a matrimonial asset in any event). The Husband had offered to return $9,000 to the pool. To take up that offer, while the Wife retained a comparable sum that she had already withdrawn, would unfairly favour her. The sensible course is to net the two withdrawals against each other. To order both to return what they took, only for the funds to be divided again in much the same proportions, would be a futile exercise that would leave them exactly where they already are.

(c)     Accordingly, I make no order in respect of the Husband's joint POSB account with the son.

Husband’s Great Eastern insurance policy (GEXXX002)

77      The asset. The Husband holds a Great Eastern insurance policy valued at $14,671.54.[note: 57]

78      The Wife's position. The Wife would like to keep the policy for the son's university expenses. It matures in February 2033 when he turns 21. Additionally, she asks to be reimbursed a sum of $30,000. She claims that in December 2013 she had given the Husband $30,000 to buy an education policy for the son. However, he had used the money instead to buy this Great Eastern policy and a Manulife policy, both in his own name.[note: 58]

79      The Husband's position. The Husband claims that the Great Eastern policy is an education-related policy bought in or around February 2015, with premiums paid from "pooled family resources". He denies that it was bought with the Wife's money, and points out that despite his repeated requests, she has produced no documentary proof of having given him $30,000 in December 2013. As he is both the policy holder and the life assured, he wants to be the sole person to manage the policy.[note: 59]

80      My decision. It is not seriously disputed that the Great Eastern policy is a matrimonial asset and is therefore liable for division.[note: 60] What the parties disagree on are, first, how the premiums were paid; and second, who should hold and manage the policy going forward.

(a)      How the premiums were paid. The Wife claims that she had given the Husband $30,000 in December 2013 to buy an education policy and that she is entitled to the entire sum. The burden of proving these claims lay on the Wife. She has not discharged it. She did not show, for instance, (i) the source of the alleged funds, (ii) that they were her personal assets, and (iii) proof that she had given them to the Husband. On the other hand, the Husband's evidence that the premiums had come from "pooled family resources" is plausible. The Wife’s claim for reimbursement of the $30,000 accordingly fails. She is not thereby deprived of the value of the policy. This is because as a matrimonial asset, the $14,671.54 will be shared between the parties in the overall division.

(b)      Who should hold the policy. The Husband is both the policy holder and the life assured. There is no good reason to incur the cost and inconvenience of transferring the policy to the Wife's name. It will therefore remain in the Husband's name.

Husband’s Manulife insurance policy (MLXXX002)

81      The asset. The Husband holds a Manulife insurance policy valued at $28,427.10.

82      The Wife’s claim. The Wife claims that she had been “actively involved in financing” this policy.[note: 61]

83      The Husband’s position. The Husband explains that he took out the policy in 1995, paid for it from his personal POSB account, and the Wife did nothing to enhance it during the marriage. He submits that it is therefore a pre-marital asset and should not be divided.[note: 62]

84      My decision. I find that the Manulife policy is not a matrimonial asset and is thus not liable for division.

(a)     The policy’s documents show that the Husband took it out in November 1995 – more than fourteen years before the parties married in March 2010. An asset acquired so long before the marriage is prima facie non-matrimonial, and the burden therefore lies on the Wife, as the party asserting that it should be brought into the pool, to prove the facts that would do so: USB at [32].

(b)     A pre-marital policy can become matrimonial asset in one of two ways. One, if it were substantially improved during the marriage, e.g., where the Wife had contributed funds to upgrade the policy. Two, if premiums were paid towards it during the marriage out of matrimonial funds. In such cases, an appropriate proportion of the policy might be included in the matrimonial pool: s 112(10) of the Charter; USB at [19(c)] and [21] to [23].

(c)     The Wife’s entire case that this pre-marital policy became a matrimonial asset rests on a single bare assertion – that she was "actively involved in financing" it. She produced no evidence to support it. For instance, she did not show (i) that the premiums were paid during the marriage, (ii) that she had contributed her own funds to the payments, and (iii) why the Husband (the sole breadwinner) would have needed financial help from her (a full-time homemaker) to pay the premiums of $62.57/month.[note: 63]

Just and equitable division

86     Having determined the value of the matrimonial assets, the next step is to divide it justly and equitably.

Parties’ position

87     The parties are not far apart on how the assets should be divided. Each accepts that the Husband should receive the larger share. They differ only on whether the Wife should have 30% or 35% share of the assets.

 

Proposed division ratio

Wife : Husband

(a)

By Wife[note: 64]

35:65

(b)

By Husband[note: 65]

30:70



My decision

88     I begin by noting that this was a marriage of about 14½ years, in which the Wife worked for the first four years before becoming a homemaker: see [31(a)] above. For a single-income marriage of this length, the precedents indicate a starting point ranging from 30% to 35% of the matrimonial assets for the homemaker spouse: see [31(b)(ii)] above.

89     The Husband submits that the Wife's share should be fixed at the bottom of that range for the following reasons.[note: 66]

… [he] was the primary financial provider throughout the marriage. [His] income formed the principal financial basis upon which the parties accumulated their matrimonial assets. While the [Wife] was employed at certain periods during the marriage, the evidence does not show that such employment constituted a substantial or sustained contribution to the acquisition of the matrimonial assets when compared with the [Husband’s] financial provision for the household.

(text in square brackets added)

90     I am unable to accept this submission. It overlooks three key points.

(a)      The same advantage has been double counted. A single-income marriage is, by definition, one in which the breadwinner made most or all of the direct financial contributions. To begin from that fact, and then to credit the breadwinner a second time for his financial support of the family, is to count the same advantage twice – and to discount the homemaker’s contributions twice over.

(b)      No credit given to the Wife for raising the son. The Husband’s submission fails to give due credit to the Wife's contributions to the marriage. In a single-income marriage, the parties contribute in different ways. One spouse earns while the other runs the home and raised the child. A homemaker’s work is not worth less merely because it is unpaid. By being a full-time homemaker, the Wife had freed the Husband to concentrate on building his career over the years.

(c)      The Wife's four years of work is ignored. For the first four years of the marriage, the Wife was employed and would presumably have contributed financially in some way to the family.

91     Next, I consider whether there are any other factors to suggest which point in the range of 30% to 35% would be a fair ratio for dividing the matrimonial assets.

92     In this regard, the Wife points to the following.[note: 67]

(a)     During the marriage, she had paid into the joint account (i) her employment income (2011–2014), (ii) the proceeds from selling her jewellery (2013–2014), and (iii) her government payouts (2014–2016). I note that there is no clear evidence of exactly how much these contributions were.

(b)     She gave the Husband (i) $30,000 in December 2013, which he used to buy two insurance policies in his own name (XXX4002 and XXX4086), (ii) $12,600 in October 2015 towards the Certificate of Entitlement ("COE") for his car, and (iii) $18,400 towards another policy.

(c)     After she was diagnosed with cancer in 2016, she remained the son's primary caregiver and continued to run the household, including being solely responsible for training the helper and buying the groceries.

93     I also consider the following submissions by the Husband.[note: 68]

(a)     The Wife's claimed financial contributions were limited, were bare assertions, and paled beside his own, which he describes as "overwhelming".

(b)     After she stopped work, the Wife had help with the household chores – her mother while she studied (2014–2016) and a helper (2017–2020).

(c)     While the Wife had cancer, he took on more responsibilities – caring for the son, managing the home, and supporting her through treatment (e.g., by driving and accompanying her to medical appointments).

(d)     He was actively involved in the son's upbringing – waking early to take him to school, attending school events, and ferrying him to enrichment and tuition classes.

94     Both parties make broad claims about their financial and non-financial contributions. The difficulty is that many of these claims lack detail and are unsupported by evidence. Additionally, many of the contributions – e.g., parenting and homemaking – are of a kind that cannot be valued in monetary terms at all.

95     This is not unusual in a marriage of some 14½ years. Most of what I am now asked to weigh happened years ago, in the ordinary course of married life. Spouses do not record every payment, every kindness, every sacrifice. Much of married life is done unrecorded, "out of love, concern and responsibility", and not with a view to building a case should the marriage fail: ANJ at [25].

96     The answer to these evidential challenges is not to treat the division of matrimonial assets as a forensic audit of a marriage. Instead, where the documents cannot establish exactly who contributed what, the court makes a "rough and ready approximation" and exercises its judgment: ANJ at [23]. In undertaking such tasks, the court considers, for instance, (a) whether each claim fits the parties' known roles, (b) the financial records that do exist, and (c) the ordinary probabilities of family life.

97     Using the above approach, two inferences are reasonable in this case.

(a)     The Wife worked for about four years, had earned a decent income, before becoming a homemaker. It is likely that she would have made financial contributions to the family along the way.

(b)     After her cancer diagnosis, the Wife's health would have been fragile. In the circumstances, the Husband would have shouldered more of the household chores and the son's care, as well as provided her with other support.

98     Weighing all of this in the round, and adopting a broad-brush approach, I find that a division ratio of 35:65 between the Wife and Husband respectively is just and equitable.

99     Applied to the total pool of $1,200,202.45, that ratio works out as follows.

The two differences ($314,583.07 and $195,416.93) come to a total of $510,000 – the value of the matrimonial home. In the words, the sole asset for division is the home. Accordingly, the home is to be sold on the open market within 12 months of this judgment.

100    The manner the sale proceeds are to be divided requires care. Let me explain.

(a)     The home was purchased substantially with the Husband's CPF monies. He withdrew $279,539.72 from his CPF towards the home: see [59] and [60] above. On a sale, that sum, together with accrued interest, must be refunded to his CPF accounts before the proceeds can be distributed. The refund is secured by the charge over the flat under s 21B of the Central Provident Fund Act 1953 ("CPFA"), which must be discharged before completion.

(b)     It is risky for the sale proceeds to be simply divided between the parties and for each to then refund his or her own CPF from the share received. This is because the Husband's required refund ($279,539.72) exceeds his share of the home ($195,416.93) by about $84,122.79. Were the gross proceeds divided first, he would have to make good this shortfall ($84,122.79) in cash to complete his CPF refund. From the evidence, it is unclear to me that he can easily top up the difference. If he cannot, the s 21B charge cannot be discharged, and the sale cannot be completed.

101    The division must be conducted in a manner that observes the CPF refund framework while achieving the same 35:65 outcome. In my judgement, this can be achieved as follows.

(a)     The net proceeds (after the costs of sale and any outstanding loan) are to be first used to refund each party's CPF accounts in full plus accrued interest. This discharges the s 21B charge.

(b)     The entire cash balance remaining from the sale proceeds after those refunds is to be paid to the Wife. This is estimated to be about $230,460.28, assuming that the matrimonial home is sold at $510,000.

(c)     Because the Husband's refund to his CPF accounts (estimated at $279,539.72) is expected to exceed his share in the home (estimated at $195,416.93), the excess funds (estimated at $84,122.79)[note: 69] are to be transferred from his CPF Ordinary Account to the Wife's CPF Ordinary Account under s 27B of the CPFA. Section 27B allows a court, in matrimonial proceedings, to order the CPF Board to transfer moneys standing to a member's credit to the other party.

(d)     The above approach ensures three things. One, that the Wife receives $230,460.28 in cash and $84,122.79 in her CPF (total $314,583.07). Two, that the Husband receives his refund of $279,539.72 less the $84,122.79 transferred out (total $195,416.93). Three, that the Wife has fresh funds in her Ordinary Account that she can use for housing and other approved purposes, and cash to meet her immediate needs as an unemployed care-parent who must now rent accommodation for herself and the son: see [110(b)] below.

Spousal maintenance

Whether spousal maintenance should be ordered

Parties’ position

102    Finally, I turn to the Wife’s request for spousal maintenance.

103    The Husband objects to this request. He submits that no spousal maintenance should be ordered because (a) he already pays maintenance for the son; (b) he has his own financial needs to meet; and (c) the Wife has recovered from her cancer and is able to work – she holds a driving licence, is a qualified early-childhood educator, and is fluent in English and Mandarin.

My decision

104    I am satisfied that spousal maintenance should be ordered.

(a)     The power to order maintenance for a former wife is directed at helping her, so far as practicable, towards the standard of living that she would reasonably have enjoyed but for the divorce, while making allowance for the new realities that the divorce has created.

(b)     Here, the Wife clearly has financial needs, and the Husband clearly has the financial means. The three objections that he raises do not preclude the ordering of spousal maintenance.

(i)        That he already maintains the son is beside the point. Child maintenance and spousal maintenance are different in nature. Child maintenance is for the son; it does nothing to meet the Wife's financial needs.

(ii)        His own financial needs go to amount, not to whether to order spousal maintenance. That the Husband has expenses of his own is relevant to how much he can fairly be asked to pay for spousal maintenance. (This is a question which I will come to below.) It is not an answer to the Wife's reasonable request for maintenance in principle. In any event, with a net income of about $7,206/month (excluding bonuses), or about $8,900/month (including bonuses), the Husband is not a man without means: see [106(d)(i)] below.

(iii)        The Wife's earning capacity. I accept that the Wife has skills and qualifications, and that she can, in time, return to work. However, it bears noting that she is now 44, has been out of the workforce for more than a decade, and is a cancer patient in remission. Given these circumstances, it is unreasonable to expect her to step back into employment overnight. She needs a period in which to re-train, find suitable work, and rebuild both her health and her working life. Her earning capacity is the reason her maintenance ought to be limited in time. It is not a reason to refuse it altogether.

Amount for maintenance

Parties’ position

105    The next issue is how much spousal maintenance is appropriate.

(a)     The Wife seeks $1,800/month. Her computation of the relevant expense items are in Annex 3, column (A).

(b)     The Husband submits that if spousal maintenance is ordered, it should be “modest, strictly time-limited and transitional in nature”. He argues that (i) $1,800/month is excessive, inflated, and unsupported by documentary evidence, (ii) the interim maintenance order of $1,700/month, which covers both the son and the Wife, is a sound guide to her financial needs,[note: 70] and (iii) her maintenance should be capped at $500/month to $600/month.[note: 71]

My decision

106    I am unable to accept the Husband’s submissions.

(a)      The interim order is not an appropriate guide. It is wrong in principle to fix final spousal maintenance by reference to the interim maintenance order. The two address "distinct factual scenarios", serve different purposes, and should thus not be conflated: XHG v XHH [2025] SGHC(A) 24 ("XHG") at [65] and [72].

(i)       Interim maintenance is provisional in nature. It is concerned with providing immediate financial support to a spouse pending the final determination of the ancillary matters hearing. Its focus is on the present need of the applicant-spouse: XHG at [65].

(ii)       Final spousal maintenance, on the other hand, answers a broader question: what does the spouse need in order to live, so far as practicable, at a standard reasonably corresponding to the marriage, allowing for the new realities the divorce has created? XHG at [66] and [72]; ATE v ATD [2016] SGCA 2 at [31].

(b)      $500/month is too low. The Husband's proposed cap of $500/month would not meet the Wife's basic personal expenses, at a time when she is unemployed. To put matters in perspective, $500/month is less than a third of what the Husband allows himself for food alone ($1,650/month): see Annex 4, s/n 5 and 6. Further, the proposed $500/month does not take into account the additional medication and health costs that the Wife, a cancer patient in remission, is likely to bear.

(c)      $1,800/month is reasonable. On my own assessment of the Wife's expenses, $1,800/month is a more reasonable figure: see Annex 3, column (C).

(d)      The Husband can afford the payment. Based on the Husband's income, he can afford to pay $1,800/month as spousal maintenance on top of the child maintenance that I have ordered.[note: 72]

(i)        His income. His employment income for Year of Assessment 2025 was $132,311.[note: 73] After the employee’s CPF contributions (about $18,360/year)[note: 74] and tax (about $6,405.54/year),[note: 75] this leaves a net income of about $107,545/year. This translates to about $8,900/month (inclusive of bonuses). If his bonuses are aside, his net income is about $7,206/month.

(ii)        The surplus. The Husband’s expenses are about $3,700/month.[note: 76] After paying $1,800/month in spousal maintenance, $800/month in general child maintenance, and about $650/month in direct child expenses, he will still enjoy a surplus. On the most conservative basis, if one were to exclude his bonuses, he will have about $256/month after paying the spousal and child maintenance. If his bonuses are counted in, the surplus rises to about $1,950/month. The Husband can plainly afford to pay spousal maintenance at $1,800/month.

Duration of maintenance

Parties’ position

107    The Wife asks for maintenance for four years to give her time to find suitable work and become financially self-sufficient. She explains that she needs long-term cancer treatment to guard against a relapse, and that the side-effects of that treatment will make it hard for her to find a job suited to her present mobility and stamina.

108    The Husband submits that one year is sufficient.[note: 77]

My decision

109    In determining the duration of spousal maintenance, the court weighs a basket of factors. These include: (a) the amount assessed to be reasonable maintenance, (b) the receiving spouse's age at the time of the order; (c) how long that spouse has been out of the workforce; (d) the spouse's education, skills and job prospects; (e) the funds that spouse receives on the division of the matrimonial assets; (f) how long the paying spouse can be expected to remain in employment. Time-limited maintenance is rehabilitative: it is meant to carry the receiving spouse across the gap between dependence and self-sufficiency. Its proper length is the time that crossing realistically takes.

110    Weighing these factors, I find the Wife's request for four years of spousal maintenance to be reasonable.

(a)      The Wife needs a realistic transition period. The Wife is in her 40s, has been out of the workforce for more than ten years, is a cancer patient in remission, and is raising a teenage son. On top of that, she will have to manage the challenging task of finding accommodation for the son and herself. Four years is a realistic period to recover her health, find work suited to her circumstances, and adapt to a new living environment.

(b)      The Wife’s monthly expenses are significant. The Wife’s monthly needs would be substantial. As a single mother renting a home for herself and a 14-year-old, she is likely to require about $4,500 to $5,450/month.
