title: YDQ v YDR
counsel: A Rajandran (M/s A Rajandran) for the Mother
The Father in person.
html:
YDQ v YDR

YDQ v YDR
[2026] SGFC 71

Case Number:FC/D 2717 of 2024 (FC/SUM 469 of 2026)
Decision Date:18 May 2026
Tribunal/Court:Family Court
Coram: Kow Keng Siong
Counsel Name(s): A Rajandran (M/s A Rajandran) for the Mother; The Father in person.
Parties: YDQ — YDR

Family Procedure – Judgments and orders – Operative date of court order – Distinction between the judicial act of approval and the administrative act of extraction of court orders

Family Law – Consent orders – Setting aside – Whether agreement to consent order vitiated by lawyer-induced pressure – Applicable considerations – Application by analogy of principles governing the retraction of a guilty plea in criminal proceedings

Legal Profession – Duties of advocate and solicitor – Duty to advise candidly – Whether a warning of cessation of legal representation amounts to coercion

Family Procedure – Conduct of proceedings – Duty not to mislead the Court when citing case authorities – Scope and content of duty

18 May 2026

District Judge Kow Keng Siong:

Introduction

1       At the close of a successful mediation, the parties in this case reached a settlement. Its terms were recorded in a draft consent order which they signed. The draft was then placed before the mediation judge, who promptly granted an "OIT" (Order in Terms). Before the order could be extracted, the Father sought to disavow the very agreement that he had just signed. Is he bound? This is the question before me.

Background

Procedural history

2       The background to this case is as follows.

(a)      Contested proceedings. The parties have obtained interim judgment for divorce; the ancillary matters remain to be resolved. Both of them were legally represented.

(b)      Draft interim order. In June 2025, the Father's lawyers informed the Court that the Mother had agreed for him to have unsupervised interim access to the children. The terms, recorded in a draft consent order signed by the Father's lawyers ("draft interim order"), are as follows.

(1)    From Monday, 23rd June 2025 to Wednesday, 25th June 2025 and Saturday, 28th June 2025 from 12 noon to 6 pm. He is to fetch and return the children to McDonalds located in Nex Mall.

(2)    He shall have alternate overnight weekend access commencing on Friday, 4th July 2025 from 7 pm to Sunday night at 9 pm. He is to fetch and return the children to McDonalds located in Nex Mall.

(3)    On the week when the [Father] does not have overnight access, he shall have dinner access on Tuesday and Thursday from 6.30 pm to 8 pm. He is to fetch and return the children to McDonalds located in Nex Mall.

(c)      Failed access. The access sessions scheduled for 25 and 28 June 2025 did not proceed smoothly. Each party blamed the other for this.

(d)      Filing of the draft interim order. In July 2025, the Father filed the draft interim order over the Mother's objection. The Court allowed the filing and the order was extracted as FC/ORC 3077/2025 ("interim order").

(e)      Enforcement application. In August 2025, the Father applied to enforce the interim order, alleging breach by the Mother: FC/ECAO 17/2025 ("ECAO application").

(f)      Setting aside application. The Mother disputed the validity and accuracy of the interim order and applied to set it aside in November 2025.

Mediation and settlement on 2 February 2026

3       At the request of the Father’s lawyers, the competing enforcement and setting aside applications were referred to judge-led mediation conducted via Zoom on 2 February 2026. The Father was represented by two lawyers assigned by the Legal Aid Bureau ("LAB").

4       The mediation ran the full day. It ended at about 6.15 pm with a settlement. The settlement terms, signed by the parties and their respective lawyers, were contained in a draft order ("draft order"). They are as follows.

(1)    Parties shall have joint custody of the two children of the marriage, namely, [Son] and [Daughter], with care and control to the [Mother].

(2)    With regards to educational and health matter, save for emergencies, the [Mother] is to inform the [Father] and the [Father] is to respond within 7 days.

(3)    The [Mother] is to bear the school fees if the children are enrolled in International School, with the educational expenses pegged to the MOE scale, to be shared equally between the parties.

(4)    The [Father] shall have 4 sessions of access, commencing on 14 February 2026 on Saturdays, from 10 am to 8 pm, and 4 sessions of overnight access from 6 pm, Fridays to 8 pm, Saturdays. The exchange shall be supervised at the FAM. There shall be a Review of access by the Court on a date to be fixed.

(5)    The [Father] shall have weekday dinner access on Tuesdays, from 6.30 pm to 8.00 pm, subject to the children not having school tests or exams.

(6)    Parties shall undertake to ensure that each of them shall maintain peace and shall conduct themselves to allow access to proceed smoothly.

(7)    The [Father] shall ensure that he sends the children for their Catechism classes (1.30 pm to 3 pm on Saturdays at [Church]). The [Mother]shall bring the children for mass on Sundays, and neither the [Father], nor the [Mother] shall ask the children about their weekend activities with the other parent or catechism or mass.

(8)    Pending facilitation of access at FAM, the [Father] shall have:

(a)    Day 2 of Chinese New Year 2026, from 1 pm to 8 pm. The pick up and return shall be at McDonald’s Restaurant at Nex Mall.

(9)    In the interim, and a (sic) without prejudice basis or until further order is made, the [Father] shall pay monthly maintenance for the children at $350.00 per month per child, with effect from 7 February 2026, and on the 7th day of each subsequent month. Such sums shall be paid into the [Mother’s] designated bank account […].

5       Two things then happened soon thereafter.

Orders made

6        First, the draft order was placed before the mediation judge, who recorded the following in the records of proceedings:

Court: After mediation, agreement reached for final orders for custody, care and control and access, and interim children maintenance.

As part of the access agreement, there shall be Supervised Exchange at the FAM.

The by consent DARO “A” (attached) has been signed by both lawyers and both parties.

1.     DARO “A” – OIT

2.    Access review and further mediation on other AMs tentatively (to confirm with parties etc.) to be fixed for 6 March 2026 …

3.    To clarify the DARO “A”, that the start date for the Tuesday access at paragraph 5.5 will be effect from 10 February 2026 by the agreement of parties.

4.    For the avoidance of doubt, by the agreement of parties, SUM 2954/2024 and ECAO 17/2025 will not yet be withdrawn pending the access review.

[emphasis added]

The Father changed his mind

7        Second, at about 6.27 pm – barely twelve minutes after mediation ended – the Father informed his lawyers that he wished to withdraw his consent. He repeated this to a court officer at 6.51 pm.[note: 1]

8       On 16 February 2026, the Father filed FC/SUM 469/2026 ("SUM 469") seeking: (a) leave to withdraw his consent to the order; (b) alternatively, that the order be set aside; and (c) a stay of any steps to extract or enforce the order.

Consent order extracted

9       On 24 February 2026, before SUM 469 was heard, the Mother extracted the order made on 2 February 2026: FC/ORC 876/2026.

Overview of the Father’s case

10     At the hearing before me, the Father advances three grounds for SUM 469.

(a)      Consent withdrawn. No binding consent order arose from the draft order because he had withdrawn his consent on 2 February 2026. He asserts that this is his “primary case”.[note: 2]

(b)      Consent vitiated. If the Court finds that a consent order did arise on 2 February 2026, then it should in any event be set aside as his consent was not freely given.[note: 3]

(c)      Consent order of limited scope. If the consent order stands, then it does not affect the interim order or the ECAO application as his consent was given on the basis that both would remain subsisting.[note: 4]

11     I address each ground in turn, beginning with the Father’s primary case.

Withdrawal of consent – Is there an enforceable order?

Key issues to be determined

12     As the applicant in SUM 469, the Father carries the burden of proof. He must persuade me that under the law, (a) a consent order is not binding until it is extracted, and (b) he is entitled to withdraw his consent before the order is extracted.

13     The Father's case raises two questions. First, when does a court order become binding? Is it only upon extraction (the Father's case)? Or it is earlier, upon the grant of an Order in Terms (the Mother's case)? Second, if the order binds at the Order in Terms stage, what purpose does extraction serve?

Applicable principles

14     The key to addressing these questions lies in understanding (a) how a consent order is made, and (b) the legal significance of each stage of its making.

15     The process begins with the parties placing their settlement terms before the Court for its approval: TOC v TOD [2016] SGHCF 10 ("TOC") at [6]; UJN v UJO [2018] SGFC 16 ("UJN") at [14]. This is done orally, or more commonly, by presenting the terms in a written draft consent order.

16     Three questions guide the decision on whether to approve a draft consent order.

(a)      Is the consent clear and unequivocal? See e.g., AOO v AON [2011] 4 SLR 1169 at [13] to [21]; UKA v UKB [2018] 4 SLR 779 at [23] and [24] (indicators of valid consent); UJN at [18] and [19] (no agreement that the order be recorded), [21] and [22] (parties' intentions unclear); XMU v XMV [2026] SGHCF 8 ("XMU") at [12] to [16] (submission of pressure rejected).

(b)      Do the terms in the draft order conform to the applicable law? See e.g., TQ v TR [2009] 2 SLR(R) 961 ("TQ") at [57], [62], [63], [67] and [103]; UJN at [16]. For instance, a parent cannot contract out of the statutory duty to maintain a child (AUA v ATZ [2016] 4 SLR 674 at [44] and [46]), and a division of matrimonial assets must not produce an outcome that is neither just nor equitable: s 112(1) of the Charter; UJM v UJL [2021] 2 SLR 1467 at [14] to [19] and [28]; XMU at [12].

(c)      Does the draft order serve the welfare of the child? The welfare of the child is the first and paramount consideration in family proceedings: s 3 of the Guardianship of Infants Act 1934. The Court will be "especially vigilant and slow to enforce agreements apparently not in the best interests of the child": TQ at [67].

17     When deciding on an application for consent order, the Court does not rewrite the order merely because it would have preferred different terms. This is because the terms are the result of a negotiated outcome. Accordingly, where the terms are unclear, unworkable, or contrary to a child's welfare, the Court will either decline to approve the draft order or invite amendments: TOC at [7]; WRP v WRQ [2024] SGHCF 12 at [5]; Seah Kim Seng v Yick Sui Ping [2015] 4 SLR 731 at [29]; Lee Min Jai v Chua Cheow Koon [2004] SGHC 275 at [16].

18     A Court will approve the draft order if it satisfies the three requirements at [16] above. Thereafter, a party can extract the order – i.e., apply for an authenticated copy of the order. The extracted order serves an important purpose: it informs third parties – such as banks, the HDB, the CPF Board – of what a Court has ordered and to secure their compliance with these orders.

19     Two principles bear emphasis.

(a)     Although a consent order has its genesis in an agreement between parties, it derives its legal force from the Court’s approval – not the agreement. Put in another way, a consent order is enforced as a court order, not as a contract: AYM v AYL [2013] 1 SLR 924 at [15] (approved in WRQ v WRP [2024] SGHC(A) 38 at [49]); Lee Hong Choon v Ng Cheo Hwee [1995] 1 SLR(R) 92 at [32] and [35].

(b)     A consent order may be set aside if the consent is vitiated by fraud, misrepresentation, mistake, duress, undue influence, unconscionability, illegality, or public policy: BMI v BMJ [2017] SGCA 63 at [5] to [7], [9]; AYM at [15] and [30]; TQ at [97]; UMM v UML [2018] SGHCF 13 (“UMM”) at [12].

My decision

A court order becomes binding once it is made

20     I reject the Father’s submission that a consent order takes effect and becomes binding only on extraction. This submission is not only conceptually flawed – it is also irreconcilable with first principles, the Family Justice Rules, and the Family Justice Courts Practice Directions 2024 ("Practice Directions"). Let me elaborate.

(a)      Conceptually flawed. The Father's submission conflates two distinct acts: the act of approving the draft order, and the act of extracting the approved order. Approval is an exercise of judicial function – the moment at which the Court makes the order. Extraction, on the other hand, is the administrative step of producing an authenticated copy by which the order is proved.

(b)      Irreconcilable with first principles. A consent order draws its legal force from the Court's sanction. The Father's reading would relocate that force from the Court's act of approval to the registry's subsequent act of extraction. This is clearly inconsistent with the principles already canvassed: see [18] and [19(a)] above.

(c)      Irreconcilable with the Rules and Practice Directions. The Family Justice Rules 2014 ("2014 Rules") apply in this case, as these proceedings were commenced before the Family Justice (General) Rules 2024 came into force.

(i)       Rule 675 of the 2014 Rules unequivocally states the moment a court order takes effect.

Date from which judgment or order takes effect

675.—(1)    A judgment or an order of the Court takes effect from the day of its date.

(2)    Such a judgment or an order shall be dated as of the day on which it is pronounced, given or made.

(3)    Despite paragraph (2), the Court may order a judgment or an order to be dated as of some other earlier or later date.

[emphasis added][note: 5]

(ii)       Rule 677(2) of the 2014 Rules contemplates that some court orders need not be extracted (or drawn up) at all.

Orders required to be drawn up

677.—(1)    Subject to paragraph (2), every order of the Court must be drawn up unless the Court otherwise directs.

(2)    An order —

(a)    which —

(i)     extends the period within which a person is required or authorised by these Rules, or by any judgment, order or direction, to do any act; or

(ii)    grants leave for the doing of any of the acts mentioned in paragraph (3); and

(b)    which neither imposes any special terms nor includes any special directions other than a direction as to costs,

need not be drawn up unless the Court otherwise directs.

[emphasis added][note: 6]

(iii)       The Practice Directions go further to state the following.

143.     Extracting an order granted by consent

(1)    Parties are not required to approve a further draft consent order after the hearing.

(2)    Solicitors need to file only the engrossed copy of the consent order after the draft consent order is granted.

[emphasis added]

144.     Unnecessary extraction of orders of Court

To reduce unnecessary documentation and to expedite proceedings, solicitors are requested not to extract orders that need not be drawn up, as provided in Part 18 Rule 3(2) of the Family Justice (General) Rules 2024.[note: 7]

[emphasis added]

(d)     To summarise: Rule 675(1) is clear – an order takes effect from the day it is "pronounced, given or made", not the day it is extracted. Rule 677(2) and cl 144 of the Practice Directions then put the matter beyond doubt: they instruct that certain orders should not be extracted at all. If it is the act of extraction that makes a court order binding, then the implication is that these provisions would be sanctioning the routine production of orders that never come into force. Such an implication is clearly untenable. The only reading that allows r 675, r 677(2), and cl 144 of the Practice Directions to be understood and applied sensibly is that an order becomes binding on approval, irrespective of whether or when it is extracted.

(e)     Finally, nowhere in the Family Justice Act 2014, the Family Justice Rules, or the Practice Directions is extraction stipulated as being required to make a consent order binding.

Wee Ah Lian

21     The Father submits that Wee Ah Lian v Teo Siak Weng [1992] 1 SLR(R) 347 ("Wee Ah Lian") is the authority for the following two propositions:[note: 8]

(a)     "[A] consent order requires the continuing consent of the parties at the time the order is made or extracted"; and

(b)     "[W]here a party has withdrawn consent before the order is made or extracted, the Court cannot proceed to record the order as a consent order against that party"

22     I reject the Father’s submission. Neither proposition appears in Wee Ah Lian. This case is concerned with an altogether different question: whether a settlement agreement had been concluded between the parties in that case by an exchange of telexes. The Court there did not address the question now before me: whether an approved consent order may be unilaterally revoked before its extraction. The Father’s reference to Wee Ah Lian is thus misleading.

Duty not to mislead the Court

23     I digress to emphasise that every litigant, including the Father, owes a duty not to mislead the Court. This is a fundamental obligation and applies to lawyers and litigants-in-person alike. This duty includes ensuring that the authority that a party places before the Court (a) is relevant to the issue in dispute, and (b) supports the proposition for which it is cited: see e.g., Bachoo Mohan Singh v Public Prosecutor [2010] 1 SLR 966 at [81]; cl 124(10) of the Practice Directions.[note: 9]

24     This duty applies even if, for instance, generative artificial intelligence tools have been used in preparing the submissions. In such cases, lawyers and litigants-in-person remain fully and personally responsible for the content in their submissions: Tan Hai Peng Micheal v Tan Cheong Joo [2026] SGHC 49 (“Tan Hai Peng Micheal”) at [32]; Family Justice Courts’ Registrar’s Circular No 1 of 2024: Guide on the Use of Generative Artificial Intelligence Tools by Court Users, (“Guide on the Use of GenAI”) at [5(1)] and [5(3)]; Tajudin bin Gulam Rasul and another v Suriaya bte Haja Mohideen at [2025] 5 SLR 518 at [101]; Goh Chin Cheng v Choco Up SG Pte Ltd [2026] SGHCR 13 at [77], [93] and [98]; XAI v XAH [2025] SGFC 93 at [14].

25     A breach of the duty is a serious matter. Among others, the responsible party may be made to pay higher costs: Forbes Monaco APAC v Kawajiri Seiji [2026] SGHC 16 at [78] and [79]. Personal costs order may also be made against a lawyer if he is the responsible party: Tan Hai Peng Micheal at [34] to [39]. See Guide on the Use of GenAI at [5(9)].

26     The duty has been breached in this case. The Father's written submissions cited Wee Ah Lian for propositions that the case did not make. By referring to that case, the Father had put this Court and the Mother's lawyer to the labour of reading an irrelevant case.[note: 10] More gravely, had the Court not read the case for itself, it might have accepted a proposition falsely attributed to that case, and this might have led to a wrong decision in the present case: Tan Hai Peng Micheal at [26] to [32].

27      The Father is now put on notice. In future submissions that he files in court, he must read every case cited therein. He must also personally verify every proposition that he attributes to a case. If a similar lapse occurs again, the Court will be firm against him and consider what steps are appropriate. I have set out these matters so that the Father knows in advance that there will be consequences if he commits another breach.

Consent order became binding on 2 February 2026

28     Returning to the issue raised in the Father’s primary case, I find that a binding consent order came into existence on 2 February 2026.

(a)     The records of proceedings show that the mediation judge was presented with the draft order on that day, which he recorded as "DARO 'A'".

(b)     The judge did not reject the draft order. Instead, he did two things. First, he recorded that the Father, the Mother, and their respective solicitors had signed the draft. Second – and decisively – he recorded an Order in Terms ("OIT") on the draft. The recording of the OIT was the operative judicial act by which the consent order was granted. It signifies the mediation judge’s approval of the draft. Read with Rule 675, the consent order took effect on 2 February 2026 – the day it was made. Nothing further was required for it to become binding.

29     Accordingly, the Father's primary case fails. He cannot invalidate the consent order by purporting to "withdraw" his consent. His remedy is to apply to set it aside on vitiating grounds: see [19(b)] above.

Vitiated consent – Is there valid consent?

The Father’s account

30     In this regard, the Father contends that his consent was not freely given for three reasons.

(a)      Advice on the interim order. First, Lawyer T had wrongly advised him that the 2025 interim order had "no practical utility". Further, the Father is aggrieved that the draft order did not expressly refer to either the interim order or his ECAO application. This omission, he contends, "fundamentally contradicted" his "long-held" position of wishing to pursue the application.[note: 11]

(b)      Threat to discharge. Second, Lawyer T had also threatened to cease acting for the Father if he did not sign the draft order. The Father claims that he told the mediation judge that he had been coerced.[note: 12]

(c)      News of son's hospitalisation. Third, the Father learned during the mediation that his son had been hospitalised with complications from recent surgery. The shock and distress, combined with the "high pressure environment" of the mediation, "severely compromised [his] ability to think clearly and [to] make a deliberate, informed decision regarding the complex terms being proposed".[note: 13]

Opportunity to respond

31     The Father has accused Lawyer T of serious professional misconduct that led to the signing of the draft order. By the time SUM 469 was filed, the lawyer had ceased to act for the Father and thus had no opportunity to address the accusation.

32     In the criminal context, where an accused seeks to retract a guilty plea by alleging misconduct against his previous counsel, fairness and proper fact-finding require that the lawyer – and any other person who may shed light on the allegation – be given an opportunity to respond: Thong Sing Hock v Public Prosecutor [2009] 3 SLR(R) 47 ("Thong Sing Hock") at [32]; Chng Leng Khim v Public Prosecutor [2016] 5 SLR 1219 ("Chng Leng Khim") at [1]; Sukla Lalatendu v Public Prosecutor [2018] 5 SLR 1183 at [21] to [24].

33     The same principle, in my view, applies where a party in family proceedings seeks to set aside a consent order on grounds of misconduct by his or her own lawyer. The two situations are closely analogous. In each of them –

(a)     The Court is asked to disregard consent that was given with the benefit of legal advice, on the basis that the advice – or the manner in which it was given – was improper;

(b)     The Court is also asked to make a finding on the lawyer's conduct, and the integrity of that finding depends on hearing more than the one-sided account of the party seeking to be released from consent;

(c)     The lawyer's professional standing may be impugned by findings made in his or her absence – a concern of basic fairness; and

(d)     The question is substantively the same: was the consent free and informed, or was it overborne by lawyer-induced pressure?

34     I therefore gave Lawyer T an opportunity to respond to the Father's allegations. I also invited Lawyer S – the Father's other counsel at the mediation – to respond if she so wished. Both accepted, and filed affidavits setting out their respective accounts of what had transpired during the mediation.

Alleged wrong advice and prejudice to the ECAO application

The lawyers’ account

35     In his affidavit, Lawyer T did not respond to the Father’s claim that he had wrongly advised that the 2025 interim order had "no practical utility". He did however state that both Lawyer S and he were aware of the Father's wish to preserve the ECAO application. They informed him that they would update the court handling the application that the intention was to observe the Mother's compliance with the consent order for a period; and if there was non-compliance, they would then proceed with the ECAO application.[note: 14] Lawyer S corroborates Lawyer T's account.[note: 15]

My decision

36     I find that the Father has not proved either that he was wrongly advised, or that his ECAO application was prejudiced. These are my reasons.

37      First, the Father is not clear about what was wrong with Lawyer T's advice that the interim order had "no practical utility". Even on the most charitable view – that the advice undervalued the interim order – he did not prove that such advice is clearly wrong. On the contrary, one can reasonably take the view that the advice is substantively correct for three reasons.

(a)      Scope. The interim order had only three operative components: (i) daytime access on specified dates in June 2025 (long since spent by the time of the mediation); (ii) alternate-weekend overnight access from 4 July 2025; and (iii) Tuesday and Thursday dinner access on non-overnight weeks. On the other hand, the draft order is considerably more comprehensive. It provides for three things not in the interim order. One, a fresh Tuesday access arrangement commencing 10 February 2026. Two, supervised exchanges at the FAM. Three, a structured review mechanism.

(b)      Duration. The interim order is provisional: it governs the children’s arrangements pending a final determination of the contested ancillary matters. The draft order, by contrast, was intended to be the parties' final negotiated agreement. The difference matters to the Father: he gains finality, stability, and closure under the draft order.

(c)      Control. According to Lawyer T, the Father's preferences were taken into account in shaping the terms of the draft order: see [55(d)] below. There is no such guarantee in the case of the interim order – it is provisional in nature and may be replaced by a judicially imposed order at the ancillary matters hearing.

38      Second, the Father's claim that the consent order has prejudiced his ECAO application is discredited by the contemporaneous record. When approving the draft order, the mediation judge recorded the following at paragraph 4 of the records of proceedings:

For the avoidance of doubt, by the agreement of parties, SUM 2954/2024 and ECAO 17/2025 will not yet be withdrawn pending the access review.

[emphasis added]

This record could only have been made at the parties’ request. This is, in substance, precisely the without-prejudice reservation that the Father now complains is absent. His claim – that the consent order "fundamentally contradicted" his "long-held" position of wishing to pursue the ECAO application – cannot stand in light of this record.

39      Third, if the Father had genuinely doubted Lawyer T's advice, or had been concerned that his right to pursue the ECAO application was not preserved under the draft order, he could have done several things. For instance, he could have (a) raised the matter with Lawyer S (his other lawyer), (b) asked his lawyers to negotiate clearer language in the draft order, (c) sought an adjournment, or (d) declined to sign the draft order. He did none of these. Instead, he signed the draft order. Such conduct cannot be squared with the claim that his consent was the product of wrong advice.

40     For these reasons, the Father's claim that he had been wrongly advised – and that his ECAO application had been prejudiced – fails.

Alleged threat to discharge

The lawyers’ account

41     I next turn to the Father's claim that Lawyer T threatened to discharge himself if he did not sign the draft order.

42     Lawyer T denies making the threat. His account is as follows.[note: 16]

(a)     At the material time, the Father had several matters pending in the Courts – a position Lawyer T regarded as "significantly prejudicial" to the Father. As a lawyer, he felt duty-bound to advise him on the ramifications of not settling.

(b)     Lawyer T also considered it important to inform the Father that legal aid could be cancelled if he did not accept counsel's advice.

(c)     Lawyer T emphasised, however, that the decision on how to proceed remained with the Father, and the latter was aware of this.

43     Lawyer S’s evidence is consistent with Lawyer T’s.

44     I accept Lawyer T’s account of the advice that he had rendered to the Father. His account is plausible and corroborated. Based on this account, has the Father’s consent to the draft order been vitiated?

Applicable principles

45     The family-law authorities do not appear to have addressed the question directly.

46     The criminal jurisprudence on an analogous situation – the retraction of a guilty plea on grounds of lawyer-induced pressure – is, by contrast, well-developed.

(a)     In Thong Sing Hock at [37], [39] and [43], the High Court held that the inquiry centres on whether the accused had the freedom to choose between pleading guilty and claiming trial. Endorsing R v Turner [1970] 2 QB 321, the Court affirmed that "[c]ounsel must be completely free to do what is his duty, namely to give the accused the best advice he can and if need be, advice in strong terms" [emphasis added].

(b)     In Public Prosecutor v Mohd Noor bin Ismail [2022] SGHC 66 at [70], the High Court emphasised that –

... lawyers are not to be passive when engaging with their clients: they should not heedlessly follow what their clients say or want without further engaging with the matter. Counsel should advise, and in doing so, may disagree with the inclinations of their clients. Certainly, if the client were to insist, counsel may need to choose whether to abide by the wishes of their client or to discharge themselves. It must be remembered that the advocate is not merely the client's unwitting or unthinking mouthpiece and should not be taking untenable positions that he cannot in good conscience advance, whilst hiding behind the veil of his client's instructions (see Prometheus Marine Pte Ltd v King, Ann Rita and another appeal [2018] 1 SLR 1 at [70]). But until that point of final resolution is reached, it is expected that counsel would consider the law, the evidence, and weigh the likelihood of success.

[emphasis added]

(c)     In Chng Leng Khim at [18], [19] and [21], the accused alleged that her counsel had informed her that (i) trial would proceed immediately; (ii) he might discharge himself for want of instructions; and (iii) she might be remanded at the Institute of Mental Health ("IMH"). The High Court held that the first two were matters which the lawyer was "well within his rights and perhaps even his duty" to raise. Only the third – the comment regarding remand at IMH which was made without proper basis – was held to have crossed the line and unfairly deprived the accused of her freedom to choose. Chng Leng Khim is relevant for the two propositions. One, a lawyer's warning that he may discharge himself is not, of itself, improper. Two, the question is whether the overall pressure deprived the client of a real choice.

47     To sum-up, three principles emerge from these authorities.

(a)      Firm advice is permitted. A lawyer is entitled to advise his client in strong terms. A warning that he may discharge himself does not, of itself, vitiate consent. Such a warning may be proper – and sometimes necessary – where the client (i) gives no instructions, (ii) insists on an untenable course, or (iii) otherwise places the lawyer in an ethical difficulty.

(b)      The operative test is loss of real choice. A warning to cease acting will vitiate consent only if, in the circumstances, it deprives the client of a real freedom to choose. Such a loss may arise where counsel (i) misstates the law, (ii) suggests an alarming consequence without proper basis, or (iii) threatens withdrawal of legal representation to coerce the client into a particular decision.

(c)      The inquiry is fact-sensitive. In determining whether consent has been vitiated, the Court considers a range of factors, including (i) why the client felt that he had no option but to consent, and (ii) why he did not instead discharge the lawyer and pursue litigation instead: Chua Qwee Teck v Public Prosecutor [1990] 2 SLR(R) 571 at [21] and [22].

48     These same principles, in my view, apply in the family-law context. Whether a party seeks to set aside a guilty plea or a consent order on grounds of lawyer-induced pressure, the operative inquiry is substantively the same: at the moment of consent, did the party have a real freedom to choose?

My decision

49     Applying these principles, I find that the Father's consent was not vitiated by lawyer-induced pressure.

50      First, Lawyer T's advice was within the legitimate scope of firm advice.

(a)     Lawyer T’s advice that the Father's pending matters were "significantly prejudicial" to him and on the ramifications of not settling is precisely the kind of firm advice that Thong Sing Hock recognises as not merely permitted, but required, of competent counsel.

(b)     Additionally, Lawyer T’s comment that legal aid could be cancelled if the Father did not accept counsel's advice is an accurate summary of the case law. This comment is also in line with the applicable statutory position. Regulation 8(1A) of the Legal Aid and Advice Regulations specifically empowers the Director of Legal Aid to cancel a grant of legal aid where the aided person has been unreasonable in the conduct of his case. Further, the LAB’s website states the following position publicly.[note: 17]

Cancellation of Legal Aid

In addition to the Director of Legal Aid’s discretion to refuse legal aid,* the Legal Aid Board may cancel the legal aid which has been granted to you, for various reasons, for example:

-    …

-    Generally, where it is unreasonable for you to continue receiving legal aid. For example, you refuse to accept your lawyer’s advice …

[emphasis added]

51      Second, the Father retained a real freedom to choose at the material time.

(a)      First, Lawyer T expressly told him that the decision was his to make.

(b)      Second, the Father’s consent was not extracted in a moment of crisis – the mediation lasted a full day and he had ample opportunity to consider his position and weigh his options.

(c)      Third, the contemporaneous records show that the Father did not provide any indication to the mediation judge that he had felt coerced at the material time. Had such an indication been given, the judge – who was bound to satisfy himself of the parties' clear and unequivocal consent before approving the draft order (see [16(a)] above) – would have been duty-bound to record this in the records of proceeding and inquire further.

52      Third, even on the most generous reading of the Father's case – that the prospect of losing legal aid had weighed heavily on him – the operative inquiry is whether he had any other option. He plainly did, as he could have pursued litigation on his own. This was precisely what he did.

(a)     After legal aid was withdrawn and within a short span of time, the Father proceeded to file six applications on his own and multiple affidavits.

(b)     These applications are not straightforward and cover diverse issues: (i) setting aside the consent order (SUM 469), (ii) compelling the Mother to allow the children to attend a church camp (FC/SUM 1152/2026), (iii) discovery (FC/SUM 886/2026 and FC/SUM 781/2026), and (iv) securing sole interim custody, care and control (FC/SUM 621/2026 and FC/SUM 620/2026).

(c)     The fact that the Father had filed so many applications demonstrate that he is not daunted by legal procedures and is adept at using legal processes to advance his interests without legal representation.

53     For these reasons, the Father has failed to discharge his burden of proving that his consent was vitiated by lawyer-induced pressure.

News of son’s hospitalisation

54     Finally, I turn to the Father's claim that the news of his son's hospitalisation and surgical complications, which were allegedly communicated to him during the mediation, had induced such shock and distress that he could not make an informed decision on the draft order.

55     I reject the claim. These are my reasons.

(a)      Distress alone is not enough; causation must be shown. The relevant inquiry is not whether the Father was distressed, but whether his distress was of such a nature and degree as to impair his ability to decide whether to consent. The burden lies on the Father to establish that causal connection. A claim of consent-impairing distress cannot be sustained on assertion alone: Chan Gek Yong v Violet Netto [2019] 3 SLR 1218 at [39] to [48]; UMV v UMW [2018] SGFC 60 at [30] to [34]; VMA v VMB [2020] SGFC 88 at [130] and [142]; XDN v XDO [2024] SGFC 88 at [43].

(b)      Bare assertion. In this case, the Father has failed to discharge his burden of proof. For instance, he has provided no evidence of his son's medical condition at the material time – such as the gravity of the post-surgical complications, the prognosis, nor any other indicator that the son’s condition was severe.

(c)      Lawyer S’s evidence. Significantly, the Father’s claim that he was incapable of giving informed consent because of the distress from his son’s condition is contradicted by Lawyer S. According to her[note: 18] –

During the mediation session, the [Father] was of sound mind, provided clear instructions to both [Lawyer T] and myself, and was not in any way distressed. Notwithstanding that he had been informed of his son's recent hospitalisation (of which he was already aware), this did not affect his ability to provide instructions or to advance counter-proposals regarding the draft consent terms on access.

[emphasis added]

Two features of Lawyer S’s account are telling. First, she observed the Father giving clear instructions and advancing counter-proposals. Such conduct is inconsistent with his alleged inability to think clearly at the material time. Second, the news was not new: the Father had already known of his son's hospitalisation before the mediation. The news was thus not the bolt from the blue that he has suggested.

(d)      The outcome contradicts the claim of impaired judgment. Finally, there is no suggestion that the Mother had leveraged on the news of the son's condition to extract an unconscionable bargain. On a plain reading, the terms of the draft order are not unreasonable. They incorporate the access arrangements that (according to Lawyer T) the Father himself had sought: (i) the resumption of weekday access; (ii) overnight access; and (iii) Chinese New Year access. These are not concessions wrung from a distressed father in a state of vulnerability; they are outcomes that he had advanced and secured. A party who obtains what he sought from a mediation cannot credibly contend that his judgment was so impaired that he could not appreciate what he was agreeing to.

Does the consent order affect the interim order and ECAO application?

56     Next, I turn to the Father's third ground in SUM 469 – namely, that even if a valid consent order came into effect on 2 February 2026, it should not affect the earlier interim order or his ECAO application.

57     The Father’s reason for raising the third ground in setting aside the consent order is difficult to understand. If he is saying that the consent order prejudices his pursuit of the ECAO application, then this is clearly wrong.

(a)      The consent order operates prospectively. The order does not retrospectively extinguish any alleged breach of the interim order occurring before 2 February 2026. It merely creates a new access regime from the date of approval.

(b)      Right to pursue the ECAO application has been preserved. As stated earlier, when the draft order was presented to the mediation judge, the Father’s procedural right to pursue the ECAO application was expressly preserved. His lawyers had put on record that they would monitor the situation, and would pursue the application in the event of non-compliance of the consent order: see [35] and [38] above.

58     Before moving on, I emphasise that nothing in this judgment expresses any view on the merits of the ECAO application. These are for the court hearing it to determine on the evidence.

Conclusion

59     For the reasons set out above, the Father has failed to prove his case on each of the three grounds advanced for setting aside the consent order. SUM 469 is accordingly dismissed.

60     Before concluding, I add some observations on the wider issues. To be clear, they are not part of disposition of the issues raised in SUM 469. I state these observations simply for the parties' future consideration.

(a)     Contested litigation is slow, costly, emotionally taxing, and uncertain in outcome. The law's preference, in family proceedings especially, is for settlement by way of a consent order. In addition to avoiding the disadvantages of contested litigation, settlement delivers outcomes that are generally more durable and better aligned with the parties' preferences for the future.

(b)     Take this case as an example. The draft order that the Father had signed on 2 February 2026 gave him the access that he had sought: see [55(d)] above. Had he honoured the bargain that he had himself helped shape, he would have spent the past three months enjoying more regular access with his children. Instead, he has spent them litigating the very order that would have delivered those arrangements. The financial and emotional toll must have been considerable. The deeper cost, though, is one that no court can give back: time with children.

(c)     In exploring settlement, the law expects every lawyer to provide candid – and if needed, firm – advice on the strengths and weaknesses of the client's case, on the realistic prospects of contested adjudication, and on the practical consequences of settling or refusing to settle. Such advice may be uncomfortable. Discomfort, however, is not duress. To set aside a consent order simply because legal advice was firm or unpalatable would be to penalise the very candour that the legal profession exists to provide.

(d)     A consent order voluntarily entered into cannot be set aside by a consenting party's later wish that he had agreed differently. The law protects the finality of consent orders. Were every consent order open to revision or invalidation because a litigant has reconsidered his preferences, no party could rely on what had been agreed, a consent order could be subject to endless litigation, and public confidence in the finality of court orders would be critically undermined: AYM v AYL [2014] 4 SLR 559 at [12]; UMM at [12].

(e)     Finally, the Father's way back to the children is not through further litigation. It is through the patient rebuilding of access and trust. In the children's interests, I hope that the Father will walk that road.

[note: 1]Reply affidavit of the Father dated 12.03.26 at [6].

[note: 2]See grounds of the application in SUM 469 at [1(g)]; Written Submissions of the Defendant/Applicant in Support of FC/SUM 469/2026 dated 09.04.26 at [2(a)].

[note: 3]Written Submissions of the Defendant/Applicant in Support of FC/SUM 469/2026 dated 09.04.26 at [2(c)].

[note: 4]Written Submissions of the Defendant/Applicant in Support of FC/SUM 469/2026 dated 09.04.26 at [2(b)] and [19] to [22].

[note: 5]Part 18, r 2(1) of the 2024 Rules similarly provides, “An order takes effect from the day that it is given unless the Court otherwise orders. [emphasis added]

[note: 6]Part 18, r 3 of the 2024 Rules contains similar provisions.

[note: 7]The comparable provisions under the 2014 Rules are P 18, r 677.

[note: 8]Written Submissions of the Defendant/Applicant in Support of FC/SUM 469/2026 dated 09.04.26 at [6].

[note: 9]Clause 124(10) states –Only authorities which are relevant or necessary for the trial or hearing may be included in the bundle. … In cases where the Court is of the opinion that costs have been wasted by the inclusion of unnecessary authorities, the Court will have no hesitation in making a special order for costs against the relevant party. [emphasis added]

[note: 10]Plaintiff’s Submissions on FC/SUM 469/2026 dated 27.04.26 at [16] to [18].

[note: 11]See grounds of the application in SUM 469 at [1(d)] and [1(f)]; the Father's supplementary affidavit dated 05.03.26 at [5] and [6]; Reply affidavit of the Father dated 12.03.26 at [9(a)] and [9(b)]; Written Submissions of the Defendant/Applicant in Support of FC/SUM 469/2026 dated 09.04.26 at [15(c)] and [26].

[note: 12]See grounds of the application in SUM 469 at [1(d)]; Reply affidavit of the Father dated 12.03.26 at [9(c)]; Written Submissions of the Defendant/Applicant in Support of FC/SUM 469/2026 dated 09.04.26 at [15] to [18] and [24].

[note: 13]See grounds of the application in SUM 469 at [1(e)]; Reply affidavit of the Father dated 12.03.26 at [9(d)]; Written Submissions of the Defendant/Applicant in Support of FC/SUM 469/2026 dated 09.04.26 at [25].

[note: 14]Lawyer T’s affidavit dated 24.04.26 at [10].

[note: 15]Lawyer S’s affidavit dated 27.04.26 at [11].

[note: 16]Lawyer T’s affidavit dated 24.04.26 at [17] and [18].
